data.title: Dollarama: Look Through A Soft Q4
data.description:
I continue to hold my shares in Dollarama (DLMAF) and maintain a "Strong Buy" rating. It is a company I have covered before and which I find is a terrific business. The company just published its fiscal 2026 Q4 results, which fell flat, with the stock down around 8%.

It is impossible to know for sure why the price moves like this day-to-day. It's better to focus on the actual fundamental performance and any commentary around guidance - and forget the stock price for a little while. There is plenty to cover on both fronts, but first a brief overview of Dollarama itself, which is not a well-known name outside Canada.

Dollarama

I have tried to portray Dollarama as sort of an “anti-Costco (COST).” Each company’s approach to retail is about as opposite as possible, and yet, both have enjoyed fantastic results. To be clear, I am a big fan of Costco - nothing wrong with it. It is just a useful contrast, with some surprising outcomes.

As we know, Costco runs massive warehouses stocked to the ceiling with a limited set of bulky SKUs. Dollarama leases small 10,000-sqft stores in heavy traffic areas, selling everything under $5 – all singles, no bundles - and usually not much parking either!

As Costco passes all savings to customers, famously keeping gross margins in the low double digits and membership fees very steady, Dollarama is a price follower, keeping all savings from its scale and thus sporting gross margins consistently around 45%.

The results? Well, it’s a case of “The opposite of a good idea is another good idea.” Since their IPO, Dollarama has outperformed even COST on a total return basis, a fact that might surprise some people:

There are some similarities in that both are actively (and successfully) pursuing international expansion – but even there, the approach differs. Costco is going about it methodically, under its own name, while Dollarama is moving quickly under the Dollarcity joint venture and, more recently, with the small-ticket acquisition of TRS in Australia.

On capital allocation, both are excellent.

What happened in Q4?

The quarter didn't look good. SSS came in at 1.5% vs. 4.9% in the prior quarter. Earnings rose a mere 0.4%. Is this a bump in the road, or is something more sinister going on?

SSS being barely positive, it adjusts to a healthier 3.5% when considering two things: i) a calendar shift that moved half a week of Halloween sales out of Q4 and substituted a full week of pre-holiday shopping (peak season) into January, a hangover month; and ii) very poor weather during peak weeks. I usually become circumspect when retailers cite the weather, but it is difficult to deny that December was particularly harsh up north.

All in all, the slow Q4 contrasts with a strong full fiscal 2026. For the year, revenues grew 13.1% and earnings per share grew 13.7%. During the call, management noted that recent weeks with normal weather show no sign of changing consumer attitudes. Guidance for the full year was in line with past guidance and shows expected growth in SSS of 3% to 4%.

Further cementing management’s confidence, capex program remains unchanged, and the dividend was increased 13.4%, matching earnings growth, also in line with past practice. Repurchases are expected to remain the go-to method for rewarding shareholders, and 2026 saw robust buybacks.

Quite clearly, Q4 is just a bump.

International Expansion Efforts

With each earnings update, we get more color on their strategy around what has become their two main international markets (by potential, if not by financial heft – yet), Australia and Mexico.

For a complete picture, Dollarama controls Dollarcity, which operates identical stores in several LatAm countries (see below).

The only "truly overseas" operation is in Australia, a business that Dollarama acquired outright.

Australia

Dollarama is in the midst of transforming the recently purchased The Reject Store. They have outlined a four-year plan to overhaul the +400 store national chain and convert them gradually into Dollarama’s format and eventually brand.

Management guided for moderate losses this and perhaps next year on this transformation. This was to be expected, as the acquisition was more like accelerating the store footprint from which to build Dollarama. Dollarama SKUs will begin showing up in Australia later this year, while new and remodeled stores are adopting the layout, dense stocking, and general Dollarama look & feel.

I like this acquisition a lot, but the payback is more into the early 2030s. Perhaps more relevant - because of its potential size - is the other new market that opened up last year, Mexico.

Mexico

Dollarcity Mexico, 80% owned by Dollarama, entered the market last year and has opened 11 stores so far. Mexico is much larger than any other Dollarcity market, with the closest being Colombia (+400 stores already). I first covered this new market here for more context.

Management declined to provide their store target for Mexico; it's too early for that. But I expect this market will become the leader in new store openings within the next two years.

For reference, Dollarama cuts the ribbon on around 60-70 stores each year in Canada, where it already has about 1,700 stores. Dollarcity, for its part, opens 80 - 100 stores per year in LatAm (excluding Mexico). Including Mexico, Dollarcity may double Canada’s new store growth in the medium term. As such, LatAm is set to become the largest growth opportunity, which is good because I am always amazed at how many stores Canada can take.

Overall strategy considerations

Canada continues to be the most relevant market for Dollarama, and that won’t change anytime soon. In Canada, the company took the long-delayed decision to build a second logistics hub to serve the Western provinces. The ensuing double-hub system will be more resilient and more efficient, further widening the moat in a market where the next largest direct competitor is 1/6th their size.

In due course, I expect Canada store growth to slow down. This will be offset by faster growth in Dollarcity and Dollarama Australia, both of which have decades of growth to look forward to. Any slowdown in Canada will imply even more free cash flow each year—which should flow to us, likely by way of even more share buybacks.

In its home market, I see the logistics hub investment—which is massive - as the final touches on their moat. The business will remain impossible to beat at home for the foreseeable future.

Dollarama valuation

You may expect to pay a premium for results that match Costco, and that is indeed the case. The premium is, in fact, lower than Costco’s and Walmart's (WMT), which is probably fair as well.

The issue with PE is that it only considers immediate earnings. Dollarama grows at least as fast as even Costco and much faster than behemoth Walmart. So we can't just look at PE without considering relative growth paths.

Because Dollarama has higher operating leverage, the earnings growth comparison is quite telling.

Therefore, Dollarama remains my top retail stock. I continue to view it as a strong buy (even more so at the current reduced price).

Risk

With international growth come incremental exposures. I estimate (based on the past 10% incremental purchase) that Dollarcity is worth only about 20% of Dollarama. But it will likely continue to grow, both because LatAm has more white space and because Dollarama still holds call options on Dollarcity. This will introduce more currency volatility, especially around the Mexican peso and Colombian peso, which are particularly subject to wide swings.

The valuation premium is also a risk. Hiccups like this last quarter will cause price drops like we saw immediately after. No issue for long-term holders who understand the business but still uncomfortable for some. I take solace in the knowledge that more shares will get bought back at low prices.

Finally, both Mexico and Australia will require a few years of losses. In Mexico because of the need to build capacity before sales catch up and in Australia because a turnaround is needed. I feel a little better about Mexico because Dollarcity is following a template that has been successful in five out of five other greenfield LatAm markets (recognizing that Mexico is more competitive).

Takeaway

The recent earnings release was surprisingly soft for the quarter, but reassuring when taking a full-year view.

Dollarama's business model has one of the best long-term potential growth profiles in all of retail and yet remains valued as if it were to grow in line with much larger industry giants. I wouldn't go as far as stating that Dollarama is exceedingly cheap; after all, every one of the three could be overpriced to some degree. But DLMAF seems reasonably priced in relation to peers and in relation to its own historical levels.

The true beauty is in the potential for growth and future cash flow generation. Should you doubt what management intends to do with that cash, I leave you with this:

data.title: CEF Insights: ASA - Opportunity In Junior Gold Mining Companies
data.description:
CEFA:

Welcome to CEF Insights, your source for closed-end fund information and education, brought to you by the Closed-End Fund Association.

Today we are joined by Axel Merk, President and Chief Investment Officer for Merk Investments. Merk Investments is the investment advisor of ASA Gold & Precious Metals Limited (ASA).

We're so glad you could be with us.

Axel Merk:

It's great to be with you again.

CEFA:

Axel, markets have seen some volatility with shifts in U.S. trade policy, as well as geopolitical tensions. Federal deficits are challenging, but the U.S. economy has been resilient. Where do you see the investment markets currently, and what is your outlook for the rest of 2026?

Axel Merk:

Thanks for asking me when the news is changing every day, it seems. For the time being, and we're recording this three weeks into the Iran war, for the time being, the market is pricing things in as a shock. And the reason I phrase it this way is because, looking out a few months, the market seems to be pricing in some sense of normalcy, whatever that might mean. The one thing I would suggest as a word of caution as to where we're going to go the shock we're experiencing is a supply shock. And the economically sound reaction to a supply shock tends to be politically unattractive. Politicians tend to do the economically wrong thing when we are faced with a supply shock. If you go to the 1970s, for example, you have an oil shock, and you react with price controls. Not that price controls are in the cards right now, but for the time being, we have extensive volatility. There are supply issues, and if you go through the noise that's extremely volatile, yields are higher, inflation might be higher, but for the most part, markets are holding up.

CEFA:

Merk Investments has specialized expertise in the gold, precious metals, and minerals sector. When we last spoke in October 2024, gold was trading around $2,650 per ounce, and now gold trades over $5,000. What is your outlook for this sector?

Axel Merk:

The price of gold has been very strong. There have been many factors ranging from central banks buying more as the dollar has been weaponized to gold bugs not just being bugs anymore, but those fringe views coming more to the mainstream as diversification buyers buy more. Interestingly, even with the shocks we've had, and gold is off its highs, gold is still hovering around 5,000. And one of the things to keep in mind is that the gold market is much smaller than many other markets. So, it doesn't need many investors to come into the space for these prices to be as strong as they are right now.

CEFA:

Where are valuations in the precious metals mining space? And do you consider these to be attractive levels?

Axel Merk:

Given where we are on the price of gold, the miners are very, very cheap, I would argue. They've obviously come up quite substantially, welding in many times multiples in the last year and a half or so. But the margins are astounding. One of the things I'd like to mention is that in 2011, Marc Andreessen said, Software is eating the world, so to speak. Now, fast forward, AI is eating software in some ways. And the big difference is that software used to be a low barrier to entry, low cost to get into that market with high margins. But now in the current world with AI, you have huge investments, and margins are questionable. The reason I mentioned this is that we suddenly have a level playing field across industries where, on the gold mining side, of course, you have very high investments, but the margins are high—50%, 60%, 80% gross margins at times. And even with the energy shock that we are experiencing, let's assume for a moment that energy prices will remain elevated for some time, that yes, energy costs can be 20%, 25% of a producer; often they're a little bit less, but there can be this sort of magnitude. Even with that, the margins are phenomenal, and the market has been very conservative in valuing the gold in the ground. And in this context, despite the very substantial volatility we are seeing, this is actually one of the places where there might be quote-unquote value in the market.

CEFA:

Axel, we mentioned the closed-end fund you manage, symbol ASA. How is the portfolio currently positioned?

Axel Merk:

We have used ASA in a way that we believe closed-end funds are designed to be used in the sense that we have focused in on smaller, often a little bit less liquid names. And when one does that, think about it as venture capital light, that as these companies grow to the extent that they succeed—of course, not all companies will succeed—there is more than just the price of the underlying metal, gold or silver or other precious metals that they're mining, but there as these companies grow and go into production, valuations tend to be higher. And so, we are aiming at taking advantage of those regardless of the price of the metal. And the history has shown since we have taken on ASA, the management of ASA since April 2019, that that strategy has worked very, very well.

CEFA:

Looking back about two years ago in March 2024, the net asset value of ASA was about $18 per share, and the fund's discount was about 15 to 16%. Here in March 2026, the NAV per share is over $80, while the discount is roughly 13%. So, the market price performance of the fund is quite similar to the net asset value performance over time. Is it reasonable for investors to consider net asset value performance as the critical driver of shareholder returns as they evaluate their long-term expectations for the fund?

Axel Merk:

Yes, is the short answer. The current price as we're talking is about $66 a share. And the discount had been narrowing for an extended period, almost every year since we started to manage the fund. The discount had been narrowing. In recent months, the discount has been widening again a little bit. But for the most part, yes, there is a very, very strong correlation, between the NAV and the equity. And of course, we are, as the advisor, focused very much on the active management of the underlying positions. And in doing so, we are trying to add value by being invested in the sector and through the active management. I'd like to add that the mining sector is the one with the greatest dispersion of returns of any of the S&P sectors. And that's a fancy way of saying that active management really matters. The junior mining space, where we are active to a significant extent, does have slightly different risk dynamics than the producers on the large cap end. And we've been trying to add value through that. And yes, the share price tends to mimic what we do in the NAV. I'd like to add that we are very transparent. We are publishing our holdings, with some delay, on a monthly basis, and we're trying to communicate what we do, and that helps to keep the NAV and the share price somewhat aligned.

CEFA:

Axel, ASA provides investors with somewhat unique exposure to junior mining companies. How important is active management for this segment of the market, and what benefits does an allocation to junior mining companies provide in an investor's diversified portfolio?

Axel Merk:

I alluded to, kind of, that we're positioned in the junior space in part because we want to have investors participate in the accretion of the value beyond the price of the underlying commodity. The value added in a diversified portfolio is directly linked to the statistical characteristics of investing in the space. That tends to be not too closely correlated to the S&P 500 or the bond market. One of the reasons the gold space has gotten more traction is because investors are somewhat disillusioned with the bond market after decades of rising bond prices and falling yields. Not that this is a direct substitute, but investors are looking for diversification, and the uncorrelated returns that ASA provides or the space in general provides has historically been a very potentially beneficial diversifier, and being able to add value with that.

CEFA:

Axel, thank you so much for taking the time to join us today.

Axel Merk:

My pleasure.

CEFA:

We want to thank you for tuning into another CEF Insights podcast.

data.title: Caledonia Mining: Gold Prices De-Risking Growth Projects
data.description:
Caledonia Mining (CMCL) is no ordinary way to invest in gold, being a single-asset miner in Zimbabwe. With 2025 results out and big plans to expand production, CMCL is worth another look. The reward is huge down the road, but miners aren't without risks in execution.

Summary of Previous Thesis

Back in December, I covered the stock not only to catch up on Q3 result but also to consider a potential policy in Zimbabwe that was related to the higher prices of gold. Namely, it was a higher royalty tax if the price of gold were to exceed $5,000 per oz. Only a proposal at the time, I didn't think too much of it, especially as gold was still below that price.

I shifted focus to the long term. Blanket mine, their lone operating asset, was providing over $200M per year with its annual production of 75K-80K oz per share. A recently completed feasibility study had shown that Bilboes could produce an additional 200K oz per year.

This could lead to $1B in revenue and perhaps $400M to $500M in free cash flow. As the market cap was $523M at the time, this would seem like a steal once Bilboes begins production in late 2028. With three years to wait and execution risks in the meantime, I observed:

As the project is a few years away too, there's plenty of room for setbacks. Nevertheless, as CMCL appreciated and increased its dividend around the completion of capital projects at Blanket in 2021, I think we can afford to wait here.

In short, I perceived no advantage in being too early on this stock, and I maintained my Hold rating.

Q4 and Full-Year 2025 Results

Caledonia just released its unaudited 2025 financial results. Both the fourth quarter and the full year proved to be one of Caledonia's best on record.

This was in spite of lower amounts of gold produced for both periods. Even though mining capacity did not decline, management attributed the lower production to the lower-grade veins they had encountered in the year. Free cash flow still grew significantly, from $10.6M in 2024 to $62.1M in 2025.

Gold ended 2024 at about $2,600 per ounce, and its price continued to climb in 2025. There was scarcely a day last year that had a lower price than one from 2024. Management acknowledged that this was the primary reason for the superior financial results of the year.

This is in spite of the fact that operating expenses (at Blanket) were up marginally for the year. The price of gold grew so much that there were fairly insignificant factors.

Bilboes and 2026 Guidance

A significant portion of the earnings call was therefore spent not discussing the results but plans and progress on the Bilboes project. For 2026, $179.8M in capex was guided for the year. $43M is for maintenance at Blanket, while $132.1M is for Bilboes.

One will likely notice that this exceeds 2025's free cash flow, high though it was. This is because management leveraged their strong balance sheet and improvements at Blanket in previous years to secure a $150M convertible note offering. After taking into account capped calls to limit their dilutive effects, they achieved net proceeds of $130M to ensure they had the cash to begin work.

Naturally, I was wondering how the higher gold price and operating cash flow it provides might influence reliance on outside capital.

Management also provided answers to that. While they expect some of this to be self-financed, they also discussed establishing credit facilities that they can use for additional draws down the road. The longer gold prices remain high, the less they will rely on this. What this indicates is that, with every passing quarter of higher prices, Caledonia's project becomes progressively less risky.

As I was present in the call, I asked CEO Mark Learmonth if any would be used for additional solar plant, to complement the one they built a few years ago, particularly as war in the Middle East raises fuel costs. He indicated that exposure to fuel costs was low and that capital improvements were being made to improve the reliability of the local electrical grid. It's a more secondary endeavor. Throughout the presentation, it was clear that extra cash is likely to be spent on Bilboes over Blanket.

Risk Factors

While the cash flow and outlook are pretty good right now, the risk factors still loom in my mind. Mining is difficult and capital-intensive, and Caledonia is still confined to the fate of Blanket until Bilboes is ready to operate.

For example, there was a fatality in September 2025, a tragedy and a setback. Another fatality occurred just the year before. For several quarters now, management has spoken to higher labor costs as a consequence of overtime, suggesting to me that the pool of capable workers for the mine is narrow. This makes a fatality particularly injurious to the mine over its lifetime, increasing the amount of hours that remaining crew must work individually.

Of course, the weakness of the electrical grid near Blanket, which was mentioned in answering my question about solar assets, is another risk factor to consider. Mines aren't just capital-intensive; Blanket is somewhat remote and exposed to vulnerabilities like this.

Additionally, the past month has shown the price of gold decline heavily after month of climbing, almost without interruption. While it's been helping Caledonia's cash flow in real time, this also reveals how quickly and easily it could reverse. The company's ability to fund its projects becomes much harder if gold prices normalize to levels comparable to 2024 or even earlier.

Valuation

As execution is the main takeaway here, it's hard for me to count too much of Caledonia's potential upside too early. At $22 per share, the market cap is down to about $414M. This may feel like a bargain, but this also largely follows the dip in gold's price. Lower gold prices fundamentally increase the risk, as it means less self-funded growth.

The company is going to be in a wait-and-see state for quite a while. CMCL could worth over $1B in the future, potentially tripling, but setbacks could easily pull this back down to $200M. The shares are in something of a middle position still.

Conclusion

CMCL at least yields about 2.5% with its $0.14 quarterly distribution. Investors get a little something, and folks who have been in it for a while could be content to wait. Those looking to take a serious position need to consider when to do it. As it will be a few years before Bilboes is operational, a flat dividend is likely. Seeing little reason to buy right now, I maintain my Hold rating.

data.title: John Hancock Multimanager Lifestyle Conservative Portfolio Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Quarterly commentary

  • Most asset categories produced positive returns in the final three months of 2025, reflecting the favorable backdrop for the world financial markets.
  • The fund posted a gain but slightly underperformed its benchmark.
  • Asset allocation contributed to performance, while underlying manager results detracted.

Market review and outlook

Global equities registered solid gains in the fourth quarter, helping the major, broad-based indexes record their third consecutive year of double-digit returns. Performance was uneven over the first half of the quarter due to concerns that AI-related stocks were in a bubble, but the market staged an impressive rebound and went on to achieve new all-time highs by year end. A continued decline in inflation enabled the U.S. Federal Reserve to enact two quarter-point interest rate cuts, boosting sentiment. In addition, corporate earnings were robust and world economic growth remained positive. Emerging- and developed-market international equities outperformed the United States, continuing a trend that was in place for the full year. Within the U.S. market, the value style outpaced growth as investors rotated toward opportunities outside of AI-related stocks.

Global bonds logged only slightly positive total returns amid a growing consensus that most central banks were largely finished easing policy. Credit-oriented market segments continued to outperform, primarily as a result of their yield advantage.

Contributors and detractors

The majority of the benefit from allocation came from the fund's overweight in developed-market international equities and corresponding underweight in the United States. We have favored the non-U.S. markets for some time based on their attractive relative valuations, and this aspect of our positioning paid off in both the quarter and the year. An overweight in equities versus bonds was a further plus. On the other hand, an overweight in U.S. mid caps detracted. The fund's fixed-income positioning had a slightly positive effect on results, with the benefit of holding emerging-market bonds outweighing weakness in the portfolio's modest holding in U.S. Treasury STRIPS. We reduced the position in the latter category over the second half of 2025.

No single area stood out as a key driver of underperformance for the fund's active managers; instead, the shortfall was primarily a result of very small differences for a wide range of individual holdings.

Portfolio changes

We retained diversified exposure across the full spectrum of the world financial markets, while also making some modest changes on the margin to capitalize on categories where we saw attractive valuations and higher long-term return potential. We remain confident in the fund's positioning as we move into 2026, based on our belief that diversification is likely to become increasingly important. For most of the past year, investors would have been rewarded by simply holding the narrow group of mega-cap U.S. technology-related stocks that continued to dominate performance. However, we began to see this backdrop evolve somewhat over the final three months of the year with a notable broadening of leadership. While it's impossible to say if this is a sign of a larger shift, we believe investors are better served by maintaining broad-based diversification rather than relying on another year of positive relative performance for a handful of companies. We think the fund is well positioned for these circumstances through its ability to seek opportunities across a wide range of asset classes.

data.title: Sixth Street Specialty Lending: Sustainable Dividend But Lacks Growth Catalyst
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered Sixth Street Specialty Lending (TSLX), I issued a hold rating at the time due to the potential recovery within the debt markets. Unfortunately, the small cuts to interest rates were not enough of a catalyst to provide relief across the BDC sector, and conditions have continued to deteriorate. TSLX reported its Q4 earnings recently, so I wanted to revisit the overall value proposition going forward. I anticipate that downside risks remain going forward unless interest rates are substantially reduced.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that TSLX's share price has declined by nearly 19%. Unfortunately, TSLX has not held up much better than the rest of the BDC market over the last few quarters. Even when including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return sits at a loss of 13.3% over the same time frame. Following the decline, TSLX now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 11.2%, and net investment income levels continue to support these payouts.

Due to the challenging market conditions, TSLX's price to NAV valuation has fallen to the lower end of its historical range. Despite this, I do not believe that it is yet an attractive time to accumulate shares. While the portfolio has remained resilient in some aspects, there are also some concerns that I have. For instance, TSLX failed to allocate a meaningful level of capital towards new investments over the last year. As a result, there aren't any meaningful growth catalysts in the near term.

Q4 Earnings

According to the latest portfolio overview, TSLX has total investments at a fair value of $3.34B that are spread across a diverse range of debt investments. Unlike traditional equities, TSLX's portfolio does not rely on the earnings growth of its positions. Instead, TSLX lends capital to middle market businesses and relies on a borrower's ability to keep up with their required debt maintenance. Approximately 96.3% of its portfolio is structured on a floating rate basis.

This means that the portfolio should technically produce a higher level of interest income from its borrowers during a period of elevated interest rates. Unfortunately, a higher cost of debt negatively impacts the spread between a borrower's earnings and the cost of its debt, squeezing margins and putting pressure on a borrower's ability to keep up with their required debt payments. On a more positive note, TSLX is most heavily invested in first lien loans, which sit at the top of the corporate capital structure and have the highest priority for repayment.

  • First Lien: 89%
  • Equity & Other: 5%
  • Structured Credit: 3%
  • Mezzanine: 2%
  • Second Lien: 1%

Expanding on this structure, TSLX maintains diversity across different industries. The portfolio is most heavily allocated to internet services at 18.3% of assets. This is followed by varying allocations to different market industries to help provide a diverse range of exposure. As we've experienced over the last few months, the indices continue to sell off SaaS companies due to the fear of AI. Despite this negative sentiment, management reaffirmed its confidence in their technology positions. However, I remain a bit cautious since sentiment in the technology sector can change quickly.

We see AI as leveling the playing field on development costs that does not fundamentally change the intrinsic moats that protect a business. Existing enterprise software companies should benefit from the shift in the cost curve if they are well managed and have limited technical debt. - Robert Stanley, CEO

TSLX reported its Q4 earnings in mid-February, and the results were a bit mixed. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.53 per share, which is now the lowest level of net investment income over the last twelve months. For instance, the prior year's net investment income landed at $0.62 per share. Total investment income landed at $108.2M, which was also on the lowest end of its twelve month range. The decline can be attributed to the lower amount of interest and dividend income that was received from its existing borrowers.

Despite the decline in earnings, TSLX's operating expenses haven't improved much over the last year. For instance, total operating expenses amounted to $56.7M for the quarter, which was only a small decline from the prior quarter's total operating expenses of $57.6M. If TSLX shifts its focus to paying down debt, management would be able to reduce its level of interest expenses over time. Similarly, TSLX has not reduced its management fees even though the earnings have declined. So there are certainly some opportunities to improve earnings through cost cutting measures going forward.

One of the biggest issues that the portfolio faces is the large amount of net realized losses. Net unrealized and realized losses totaled $20.5M for the quarter, which was a large increase from the prior quarter's total net realized loss of $6M. This is an indication that the underlying positions are either struggling under the pressure of higher rates or management is implementing portfolio shifts to improve the efficiency of earnings.

One important metric for TSLX is how it will manage the upcoming debt maturities. TSLX has $300M in unsecured debt and another $46M drawn revolver maturing this year. The $300M must be repaid or refinanced, while the $46M revolver might be able to be rolled forward. If management is able to successfully navigate the $300M in debt, I anticipate that performance will start to improve through 2027 since there is no debt maturing then.

Valuation & Risk Profile

At the time of my last coverage, TSLX traded at a large premium to NAV of 30.22%. Following the recent decline in share price, TSLX now trades at a very small premium to NAV of 4.53%. For reference, TSLX has traded at an average premium to NAV of 24.83% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that TSLX now trades at the lower end of its historical price-to-NAV range. Despite the discount, I do not believe this is a good time to accumulate shares due to the threat of higher interest rates eroding earnings over time.

An indication that TSLX is struggling to grow its base assets was the failure to allocate sufficient capital towards new investments over the last year. For the entire year of 2025, TSLX was able to achieve $894M in total investment fundings. While this was in line with its typical range, it was offset by sales and repayments of $1.19B for the year. This resulted in a net investment activity of negative $302M for the year.

When we combine the declining earnings with the failure to grow its portfolio through new investments, the underlying NAV has declined. A declining NAV is a clear indication that changes need to be implemented to improve the portfolio going forward. For reference, the quarter-end NAV amounts are as follows:

  • Q4 2024: $17.16 per share
  • Q1 2025: $17.04 per share
  • Q2 2025: $17.17 per share
  • Q3 2025: $17.14 per share
  • Q4 2025: $16.98 per share

Unfortunately, I do not believe that there will be significant improvement to the rate of capital that can be allocated towards new investments in the near-term. The Fed has decided to leave interest rates unchanged during their latest meeting, which means that the cost of debt remains relatively high. Forward looking, I believe there are two possible outcomes from this point if interest rates remain high.

  1. Management can refine its existing portfolio and continue to navigate market conditions. The portfolio may not thrive, but it can survive.
  2. The higher cost of debt may continue to slowly erode earnings and increase the rate of non-accruals within TSLX's portfolio.

Conversely, reduced interest rates have the potential to serve as a positive growth catalyst. However, it really depends on how large the Fed's future cuts are; a smaller cut of 25 basis points may not be enough to really drive the needle forward for growth. However, a larger set of cuts may lead to an influx of new borrowers seeking debt financing, which would provide management with an opportunity to capitalize on a higher rate of new investment activity.

Non-accruals remain relatively low at 0.6% of fair portfolio value, which remains unchanged from the time of my last coverage. The good news is that borrowers on non-accrual status make up a small portion of total assets. For instance, one new borrower was added to non-accrual status during the quarter, Alkagen, which only represents 0.01% of TSLX's fair portfolio value.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.46 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.2%. As previously mentioned, net investment income amounted to $0.53 per share for the quarter. This represents a dividend coverage rate of 115% and should instill confidence that management will be able to sustain payouts over time. Furthermore, management confirmed that they still have $1.21 per share worth of undistributed spillover income available. Although spillover income can serve as a buffer to lower earnings, the usage will reduce the underlying NAV over time.

Despite the weaker earnings, TSLX has demonstrated its ability to provide some resilience during these challenging conditions. Combined with the excess of spillover income, I believe that TSLX continues to be a solid choice among the BDC sector for investors looking to secure a reliable source of dividend income.

Some good news is that the tax-efficiency of BDCs may increase as a result of the One Big Beautiful Bill. This bill allows 'qualified' production activities and pass-through income structures to receive a discount on the effective tax rate. It will essentially allow the distributions from BDCs to have a similar classification as the dividends received from REITs. Despite this, I still believe that TSLX is best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to avoid any complicated tax burdens.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain a hold rating on TSLX at this time. Despite the deeper discount to NAV valuation, I don't think there are enough growth catalysts to justify the accumulation of shares yet. At the same time, I also don't think there are many red flags that are a cause for concern yet, so existing shareholders may remain invested and collect a reliable stream of dividend income through 2026. There is a sizeable level of spillover income that can be tapped into, and the portfolio has a low non-accrual rate, which indicates that the portfolio remains relatively healthy in this higher interest rate environment.

data.title: Virtus Convertible Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
General Market Environment

Equities, convertible securities, and high yield (HY) bonds all advanced in the fourth quarter. Third-quarter earnings results beat expectations on strong artificial intelligence (AI) spending, though management commentary highlighted cost pressures and uneven demand trends. Economic data was mixed, and visibility was impacted by the government shutdown; labor market indicators softened, while consumer spending and inflation metrics were stable. The U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) cut rates by 25 basis points in both October and December and signaled further accommodation with markets pricing additional cuts into 2026. Against this backdrop, the 10-year U.S. Treasury yield finished modestly higher at 4.17% despite significant intra-quarter volatility.

Convertible Securities Market Environment

  • The ICE BofA U.S. Convertible Index returned 1.97% for the quarter, bringing full-year performance to 17.98%.
  • Sector performance was mostly higher with consumer staples, industrials, and healthcare outperforming, while financials, consumer discretionary, and media underperformed.
  • Below-investment grade issues outperformed investment grade issues. Equity sensitive issues outperformed yield-oriented (busted) and total return (balanced) issues.
  • New issuance saw 46 issues priced, raising $36.2 billion in proceeds, bringing the full-year total to a record $118.8 billion.

Portfolio Commentary

Sector exposures that contributed to relative performance in the period included technology, healthcare, and consumer discretionary. Within technology, both security selection and asset weighting decisions contributed to relative performance across all subindustries, with an industry underweight in software having the largest impact. Positive selection within health services and medical products, along with overweight positioning in pharmaceuticals, were the primary drivers of outperformance in healthcare. Relative strength in consumer discretionary was attributable to favorable security selection within automakers and theaters & entertainment.

Sector exposures that detracted from relative performance in the period included industrials, materials, and telecom. In industrials, negative security selection and overweight positioning within aerospace detracted the most from relative performance, with an additional adverse impact from selection in machinery. Weakness in the materials sector was attributable to an underweight in the outperforming chemicals industry. Both overweight positioning and security selection in satellite telecom negatively impacted broader telecom performance, with additional relative detraction from selection within wireline telecom.

Exposure increased the most in healthcare, utilities, and consumer discretionary, and decreased the most in technology, industrials, and energy.

The conversion premium at quarter-end was roughly 30%, and we believe the strategy is well positioned to participate in the upside and mitigate on the downside.

data.title: Silver And Gold Intraday Outlook: The Metals Space Is As Confused As Traders Are
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Today is an unusual session for the markets following a chaotic day yesterday. Participants cannot be blamed for the current uncertainty; we are now in the aftermath of a series of decisions made by major central banks. Fundamentals are inconsistent, leading to a widespread sense of confusion across all asset classes, resulting in a slow consolidation.

Given that many of the world's most educated economists, like the Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell and other central bankers, seemingly struggle to understand the ongoing situation, it will be challenging to predict the market's direction once the uncertainty lifts.

Metals are not immune to this confusion, especially after experiencing volatile 10% swings up and down yesterday. They are today, however, experiencing a relatively calmer session: silver is up a shy 1%, gold retreats from $4,400 and other metals like platinum are hanging close to unchanged.

In general, after periods of significant volatility and a lull in news, the markets tend to trade within a range. This is precisely what we are witnessing today. The best approach now is to take a step back and prepare for what lies ahead. These types of sessions offer key breakout levels, as algos and traders await news to catalyze the next movement in the markets.

Let's explore the recent shifts in an intraday timeframe analysis of gold (XAU/USD) and silver (XAG/USD) to identify where the key levels to watch for breakouts are.

Gold (XAU/USD) 4H Chart and levels

Gold is now stuck in a triangle formation right at its quintessential $4,400 level – decisive for upcoming action. Remaining with +/- $75 of the psychological zone gives a further sense of uncertainty; hence, this provides key breakout levels to play as the week continues:

  • Above $4,475, bulls will hold the advantage until $4,700
  • Below $4,325, however, expect $4,100 to retest swiftly

Intraday timeframe levels to watch for gold (XAU/USD):

Resistance Levels:

  • Short-term breakout level: $4,475
  • February wick pivot: $4,675-4,725
  • $4,850 to $4,900 key resistance
  • $5,100 pivotal resistance
  • $5,400 mini resistance

Support Levels:

  • Pivotal support: $4,325-4,400
  • Main channel lows support $4,100
  • Higher timeframe support: $3,880 to $4,000
  • $3,200 to $3,500 major support

Silver (XAG/USD) 4H Chart and levels

Silver has seen similar volatility and is now fighting to hold its $70 key level (+/- $1).

  • Rebounding and closing on a 4H candle above $71 would point to a swift retest of the $75 to $77.50 momentum pivot level, re-entering its 2025 bull channel
    • Extending above $80 puts back the trend in the bull's hand
  • Rejecting $69, however, would point to a retest of the $62 previous session support.
    • Breaking below $61 hints at the $50 to $55 October 2025 support

Resistance Levels:

data.title: Richmond Manufacturing Index: Activity Was Flat In March
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Fifth district manufacturing activity was flat in March, according to the most recent survey from the Federal Reserve Bank of Richmond. The composite manufacturing index rose ten points to 0, marking the first nonnegative reading in over a year. This month's reading was above the forecast of -8.

Here is an excerpt from the latest Richmond Fed manufacturing report:

All three of its component indexes rose in March. Shipments increased to −2 from −13, new orders improved to 4 from −9, and employment rose to −2 from −7.The local business conditions index increased to −5 in March from −15 in February. Meanwhile, the future local business conditions index decreased but remained in positive territory. The future indexes for shipments and new orders also decreased slightly but remained solidly in positive territory. The expectations index for employment rose to 14 from 6.The average growth rate of prices paid decreased somewhat, while average growth in prices received increased in March. Firms expected growth in both price measures to moderate over the next 12 months.

Background on Richmond Fed Manufacturing

The complete data series behind today's Richmond Fed manufacturing report, which dates from November 1993, is available here. The Richmond Manufacturing Index is a gauge of manufacturing activity in the Fifth Federal Reserve District (Maryland, North Carolina, the District of Columbia, Virginia, most of West Virginia, and South Carolina) compiled from a survey of ~100 manufacturers.

The composite manufacturing index is an average of indexes on shipments, new orders, order backlogs, capacity utilization, supplier lead times, number of employees, average workweek, wages, inventories, and capital expenditures.

This is a diffusion index, meaning negative readings indicate contraction and worsening conditions, while positive ones indicate expansion and improving conditions. The survey offers clues on inflationary pressures and the pace of growth in the manufacturing sector for this region of the country, and the accumulated results can help trace long-term trends.

Here is a snapshot of the complete Richmond Fed Manufacturing Composite series.

For comparison, here is the latest ISM Manufacturing survey.

Let's compare all five regional manufacturing indicators. Here is a three-month moving average overlay of each since 2004 (for those with data).

Here is the same chart including the average of the five.

data.title: Templeton Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Key Takeaways

  • Markets: Global equity markets collectively posted moderate gains in the fourth quarter of 2025, marking a period of consolidation after the stronger advance seen in the third quarter.
  • Contributors: Stock selection and an overweight in the health care sector, stock selection in the utilities sector and stock selection and an underweight in the consumer discretionary sector contributed to relative performance for the quarter.
  • Detractors: Stock selection and overweights in the industrials, materials and communication services sectors detracted from relative performance for the quarter.
  • Outlook: We remain broadly constructive on the longer-term outlook for equity markets, but the strength of 2025's rally suggests that some caution is appropriate in the coming months.

Performance Review

  • Global equity markets collectively posted moderate gains in the fourth quarter of 2025, marking a period of consolidation after the stronger advance seen in the third quarter. The fourth quarter unfolded against a backdrop of easing—but uneven—monetary policy expectations, greater stock-return dispersion across regions and sectors, and a rotation away from the most crowded growth investment themes. October experienced an extension of the post-summer stock rally as interest-rate cuts and generally resilient earnings supported risk appetite; November brought a pause as investors reassessed equity valuations and monetary policy timing; and December witnessed a stabilization of sentiment as central banks delivered broadly dovish outcomes while flagging increased data dependence into 2026.
  • The fund (Advisor Class without sales charges) outperformed its benchmark, the MSCI All Country World Index-NR, for the quarter, driven by stock selection and an overweight in the health care sector, stock selection in the utilities sector and stock selection and an underweight in the consumer discretionary sector.
  • Eli Lilly's shares rose during the quarter as investors responded to continued strength in demand for its glucagon-like peptide-1 (GLP-1) obesity and diabetes treatments. The company reached a significant milestone during the quarter, becoming the first pharmaceutical firm to surpass a $1 trillion market capitalization on November 21. Sales of Mounjaro and Zepbound remained the key drivers of investor sentiment, supporting expectations for sustained growth across Lilly's metabolic health portfolio. That performance reinforces our investment thesis. Lilly's leadership in GLP-1 therapies positions the company to capture a large, structurally growing market, and we expect continued revenue and profit-margin expansion driven by Mounjaro and Zepbound, manufacturing scale and a deep R&D (research and development) pipeline. We view a premium valuation multiple as reasonable if Lilly delivers the 20%-plus revenue growth we expect over the next few years and converts pipeline optionality, including Orforglipron, into clinical and commercial progress. We continue to monitor the company's competition, regulatory and execution risks, but the combination of sustained commercial momentum and operational advantages supports our conviction in Lilly as a core growth holding.
  • Stock selection and overweights in the industrials, materials and communication services sectors detracted from relative performance for the quarter.
  • The shares of Universal Music Group (UMGNF) fell due to disappointing financial results. However, the company is revamping its streaming model to boost pricing control and profit margins, improve predictability and strengthen its market position. Better communication and potential US listing could add upside to the stock, and we view the company as having solid long-term growth prospects.
  • Regionally, stock selection and an underweight in the United States significantly contributed to relative performance for the quarter. Stock selection in the eurozone detracted from relative results.

Outlook

  • We remain broadly constructive on the longer-term outlook for equity markets, but the strength of 2025's rally suggests that some caution is appropriate in the coming months.
  • European equities' fourth-quarter outperformance reflected both structural sector advantages and a valuation buffer relative to US equities. Political and fiscal noise persisted, particularly in France, but the region's equity markets remained relatively insulated. Stocks were supported by a combination of defensive earnings profiles, sector composition, and improving policy clarity. Despite persistent skepticism, we believe the European economy looks poised for upside surprises in 2026.
  • In Japan, the recent change in government has reinforced the policy backdrop for reform and long-term growth. Japan has begun to lift interest rates for the first time in decades, which signals the return of inflation and a normalization of economic conditions after a prolonged deflationary period. This environment is particularly constructive for Japanese banks, which we believe could benefit from steeper yield curves and improving profitability. In our view, a gradual recovery in the yen, following an extended period of weakness, could also support domestic sectors.
  • Asia more broadly continues to benefit from a weaker US dollar and improving underlying momentum. We continue to find attractive bottom-up opportunities and expect to increase exposure as opportunities develop.

data.title: Columbia Select Short Corporate Income Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

  • Columbia Select Short Corporate Income Fund Institutional Class shares returned 1.03% for the three months ending December 31, 2025.
  • The fund's primary benchmark, the Bloomberg U.S. 1–5 Year Corporate Index, returned 1.26% for the same period.

Market overview

The fourth quarter in the United States was marked by significant economic data collection challenges stemming from the longest government shutdown in U.S. history. Running from October 1 to November 12, the 43-day shutdown surpassed the previous record of 35 days in 2018–2019, creating substantial complications for interpreting economic data and assessing the state of the U.S. economy.

The fourth quarter was characterized by relatively low volatility in U.S. financial markets, with machinations mostly tied to swings in sentiment related to the artificial intelligence (AI) capital expenditure cycle. Returns were relatively muted. The S&P 500 Index rose 2.66% during the fourth quarter and 17.88% for the full year. The Nasdaq Composite gained 2.57% for the quarter and 20.17% for the year, while the Russell 2000 Index advanced 2.19% for the quarter and 12.81% for the year,

On the economic data front, the labor market showed continued cooling during the fourth quarter, with the unemployment rate rising to 4.6% in November (up from 4.4% in September), a four-year high. Nonfarm payrolls increased by just 64,000 in November, following a 105,000 decline in October, the largest decline since the end of 2020. The October payroll contraction was driven primarily by a 162,000 reduction in federal government employment, as workers who accepted the Trump administration's deferred resignation offers officially dropped off payrolls. Stripping out government job losses, private payroll growth remained in a relatively narrow range, recording 52,000 in October and 69,000 in November, with the unemployment rate increase attributable to those DOGE-related federal workforce reductions.

The Fed reduced the target range for the federal funds rate by 25 bps to 3.50%–3.75% at its December meeting, despite facing what Chairman Powell described as "dual mandate tension," with upside risks to inflation and downside risks to employment creating a challenging policy environment. The December decision was far from unanimous, with only nine of twelve members supporting the cut, including two official dissents and four soft dissents. Chair Powell acknowledged this unusual division, noting that persistent tension between the two parts of the mandate naturally produces such disagreement, with differences centered on how to weight competing risks, rather than disagreement about the underlying conditions. "Everyone around the table at the FOMC agrees that inflation is too high, and we want it to come down, and agrees that the labor market has softened and that there is further risk. Everyone agrees on that. Where the difference is, is how do you weight those risks...It's very unusual to have persistent tension between the two parts of the mandate. And when you do, this is what you see."

The Bloomberg U.S. Corporate Investment Grade Index (index) generated 0.84% of total return and -0.04% of excess returns (the impact of spread widening or tightening plus carry) in the fourth quarter. For the full year 2025, the index generated 7.77% total return and 1.19% of excess return.

Industrials, utilities and financials generated excess returns of -0.17%, 0.06% and 0.14%, respectively, in the fourth quarter. Mid-rated credits outperformed, as some idiosyncratic issues affected certain credits and sectors differently. For the fourth quarter, A rated corporates (0.04%) outperformed BBB rated corporates (0.07%), which outperformed AA rated corporates (-0.31%) and AAA rated corporates (-0.40%) on an excess return basis.

Outperforming industries over the quarter (from an excess return perspective) included transportation services, automotive, lodging, office REITS and airlines. The worst-performing industries over the quarter included cable satellite, media entertainment, technology, chemicals and wirelines.

From an issuance perspective, per Barclays index data, the fourth quarter of 2025 saw approximately $321 billion of U.S. dollar-denominated investment-grade corporate and noncorporate issuance, a 35% increase from the fourth quarter of 2024. The fourth quarter capped a year of robust new-issuance activity, with record monthly supply in both October and November pushing total 2025 issuance of $1.82 trillion almost 4% above 2024 levels. Total 2025 issuance was the second-highest issuance year, trailing only the 2020 COVID-impacted issuance record of $2.21 trillion.

Attribution

  • An overweight to credit risk, as measured by contribution-to-spread duration, marginally added to portfolio performance.
  • Security selection had a negative effect on performance over the quarter. The largest detraction came from an allocation to a technology company.
  • Industry allocation did not have a material effect on performance.
  • Duration, convexity and curve positioning detracted from overall performance.

Outlook

As we enter 2026, fundamentals for the investment-grade corporate market remain very solid. The Quarterly U.S. Investment-Grade Corporate Outlook for the fourth quarter of 2025, recently published by our Columbia Threadneedle Investments credit analyst partners, shows improvement in aggregate across various measures relative to the third-quarter estimates. The outlook for 2026 is also showing improvement. Expectations for revenue growth are slightly higher, EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization) margins in aggregate are expected to expand slightly and capital expenditures were revised significantly higher, with a large step up in spending in the information technology sector and no changes to net leverage estimates — although investors are still expecting a slight decline year over year. However, spreads are starting 2026 at historically tight levels, with the widest spreads of the past six months representing the lowest six-month rolling maximum in history. This suggests very little upside potential from current levels.

Client portfolios enter 2026 in a similar position from a duration-times-spread (DTS) perspective, compared to where they started the fourth quarter — namely, essentially flat versus their respective benchmarks. Portfolios have ample liquidity to participate in what we expect to be a robust new-issuance calendar as we move into 2026 — issuance tied to AI-related capex and M&A-related financing. We believe the idiosyncratic nature of this issuance will lead to an increase in dispersion among issuers and sectors and could reshape credit curves with a bias to steepen, in our view, given expectations for a continuation/pickup in the percentage of longer-maturity issuance. We believe this type of market will favor our research-intensive, bottom-up security-selection investment process much more so than the market we have experienced of late, where macroeconomic factors primarily drove spread movements (beta) and there was a notable lack of dispersion (alpha).

Market risk may affect a single issuer, sector of the economy, industry or the market as a whole. Fixed-income securities present issuer default risk. A rise in interest rates may result in a price decline of fixed-income instruments held by the fund, negatively impacting its performance and NAV. Falling rates may result in the fund investing in lower yielding debt instruments, lowering the fund's income and yield. These risks may be heightened for longer maturity and duration securities. Non-investment-grade securities (high-yield or junk bonds) are volatile and carry more risk to principal and income than investment-grade securities. Prepayment and extension risk exists because the timing of payments on a loan, bond or other investment may accelerate when interest rates fall or decelerate when interest rates rise which may reduce investment opportunities and potential returns. As a non-diversified fund, fewer investments could have a greater effect on performance. Foreign investments subject the fund to risks, including political, economic, market, social and others within a particular country, as well as to currency instabilities and less stringent financial and accounting standards generally applicable to U.S. issuers. Risks are enhanced for emerging market issuers. Market or other (e.g., interest rate) environments may adversely affect the liquidity of fund investments, negatively impacting their price.
